_meta.unit: Theories wave-2 Jon Snow resurrection / second-life / Azor Ahai-destiny cluster
_meta.note: Jon Snow resurrection cluster: mint jon-snow-resurrection (concept.theory) for the second-life-in-Ghost / cost-of-resurrection / physical-transformation mechanic, and enrich the existing azor-ahai-theories node with beats that add genuinely new evidence beyond its S216 Z7/Z8/Z9 Jon-candidacy edges. Source: ASX video qSy2uaJ7ecU ('The real Jon Snow'), theory header 'Jon's resurrection, second life, and Azor Ahai destiny' (line 3 of working/theories/extractions/qSy2uaJ7ecU.jsonl), substrate working/theories/jonsnow-cluster/substrate.jsonl (22 byte-verified beats). All quotes byte-verified against sources/chapters/*.md at cited lines during this session; six beats (B38/B41/B43/B48/B56/B73) carried verbatim_quote:null in the substrate and were independently extracted+verified from their cited file:line during this pass. JR8 (adwd-jon-01.md:317) was found while verifying B48's chapter and is not itself a substrate row -- added because it directly strengthens the same beat with an independently byte-verified companion quote.

edges.quote: In the cold night air the wound was smoking. “Ghost,” he whispered.
edges.note: SUBJECT-LINK per layer rule: Jon's dying word as he bleeds out during the Castle Black mutiny is the direct textual seed for the whole second-life-in-Ghost theory. Sourced from the tier-1 event node whose confirmed textual content anchors the theory, per the assassination-scene guidance. Found independently while verifying B48's chapter (not itself a substrate row) -- the single most load-bearing quote in the cluster.

edges.quote: “A dream,” said Jon feebly. “I was Ghost, I was on the edge of the mountain looking down on a frozen river, and something attacked me. A bird . . . an eagle, I think . . .”
edges.note: Precedent: when the skinchanger Orell dies, his spirit lives on inside his eagle, which then stalks and attacks Jon in revenge -- direct textual proof skinchanger spirits survive bodily death with retained agency (substrate B37).

edges.quote: Mance should have let me take the direwolf. There would be a second life worthy of a king.
edges.note: Varamyr, dying, names Ghost explicitly as a 'second life worthy of a king' for Jon -- the theory's own title phrase, stated in-text (substrate B38, independently extracted; substrate carried verbatim_quote:null).

edges.quote: When the man’s flesh dies, his spirit lives on inside the beast, but every day his memory fades, and the beast becomes a little less a warg, a little more a wolf, until nothing of the man is left and only the beast remains.
edges.note: Haggon's skinchanger lore (recalled by Varamyr) establishes the second-life mechanic itself AND its risk -- the longer Jon's spirit stays in Ghost, the more he risks losing his humanity entirely (substrate B41, independently extracted; substrate carried verbatim_quote:null). Double-edged: supports the mechanic's existence while also gating how the theory node should read its outcome as unresolved/risky, not guaranteed-safe.

edges.quote: Now he was a man, now a wolf, now a man again.
edges.note: Melisandre's fire-vision of Jon oscillating between man and wolf states -- read as a direct prophetic image of his spirit's sojourn in Ghost before his human body revives (substrate B39).

edges.quote: No, he thought. I am a man, not a wolf.
edges.note: Jon's habitual, deliberate suppression of his wolf-dream/skinchanger side; the theory reads his death as the event that finally breaks this self-restraint (substrate B43, independently extracted; substrate carried verbatim_quote:null). Interpretive: the text shows only present-day restraint, not a post-death prediction.

edges.quote: Winter is almost upon us now. I am your only hope.
edges.note: Melisandre names herself Jon's 'only hope' in the same chapter he is killed minutes later -- read as foreshadowing that she will be his resurrector (substrate B48, independently extracted; substrate carried verbatim_quote:null).

edges.quote: This is my place as it is yours, and soon enough you may have grave need of me.
edges.note: Companion line to JR7 from an earlier chapter -- Melisandre repeatedly signals across the book that Jon will need her at a moment of crisis. NOT a substrate row: found and independently byte-verified while checking B48's citation (the B48 paraphrase referenced both the 'only hope' and 'grave need of me' lines, but the substrate only cited the former's location). Flagged for orchestrator review as an addition beyond the assigned substrate.

edges.quote: “Six,” Thoros said reluctantly. “And each time is harder. You have grown reckless, my lord. Is death so very sweet?”
edges.note: Alternative no-death-required resurrection precedent: Thoros has revived Beric six times via R'hllor's power without anyone else dying, but Beric loses more memory/identity each time -- suggests Jon's own resurrection cost could be his sense of self rather than someone else's life (substrate B52).

edges.quote: Her hair was dry and brittle, white as bone. Her brow was mottled green and grey, spotted with the brown blooms of decay.
edges.note: Lady Stoneheart precedent for post-resurrection physical transformation (white hair, disfigurement) -- offered as a template for how Jon's own body/appearance might change after revival (substrate B53).

edges.quote: “The crow, the crow,” Patchface cried when he saw Jon. “Under the sea the crows are white as snow, I know, I know, oh, oh, oh.”
edges.note: Patchface's fool-riddle, read as a hint of a future pale, white-haired, undead Jon (substrate B56, independently extracted; substrate carried verbatim_quote:null). Interpretive -- riddle-reading, not a stated prophecy.

edges.quote: Lightbringer was his sword. Tempered with his wife’s blood if Votar can be believed. Thereafter Lightbringer was never cold to the touch, but warm as Nissa Nissa had been warm.
edges.note: The legendary Azor Ahai forged Lightbringer by sacrificing his wife Nissa Nissa -- the textual sacrifice-precedent for what 'forging the real Lightbringer' might cost Jon. Sourced from the tier-1 legend node per the layer rule (substrate B64; B75 is an adjacent substring of the SAME line describing the monster-slaying aftermath -- redundant, not separately edged; see PROPOSAL.md dedup notes).

edges.quote: So as cold and death filled the earth, the last hero determined to seek out the children, in the hopes that their ancient magics could win back what the armies of men had lost. He set out into the dead lands with a sword, a horse, a dog, and a dozen companions.
edges.note: Old Nan's Last Hero legend -- a sword, a dog, and companions setting out into the dead lands to defeat the Others with the children of the forest's aid -- offered as the in-world template for Jon's own eventual journey north with Ghost and a company of rangers (substrate B73, independently extracted; substrate carried verbatim_quote:null; substrate's own paraphrase said 'a sword, a dog, and companions' but the text also includes 'a horse' -- quoted in full here for accuracy). Sourced from the tier-1 legend character node.

edges.quote: “He has a song,” the man replied. “He is the prince that was promised, and his is the song of ice and fire.” He looked up when he said it and his eyes met Dany’s, and it seemed as if he saw her standing there beyond the door. “There must be one more,” he said, though whether he was speaking to her or the woman in the bed she could not say. “The dragon has three heads.”
edges.note: House of the Undying vision: the harp-playing man (implicitly Rhaegar) names the newborn 'Aegon' 'the prince that was promised' with 'the song of ice and fire' and adds 'the dragon has three heads' -- all in one witnessed moment (substrate B66). DEDUP-CHECKED against the existing azor-ahai-theories edge set: distinct from Z10 (acok-daenerys-05.md:111, Dany's LATER RECOLLECTION of this same vision to Ser Jorah) -- this is the primary vision scene itself and includes the three-heads line Z10 lacks. Routing 'Aegon' to Jon requires the separate R+L=J assumption; interpretive.

edges.quote: Stannis drew the blade he called Lightbringer. “Here is your sword in the darkness.” Light rippled up and down the blade, now red, now yellow, now orange, painting the king’s face in harsh, bright hues.
edges.note: Jon personally witnesses Stannis's glowing 'Lightbringer' -- the scene the video reads as planting Jon's own skepticism about the Stannis candidacy, clearing narrative space for a different Azor Ahai candidate (substrate B61). DEDUP-CHECKED: new witness/angle (Jon's own POV) beyond the existing Z2/Z3/Z4 Stannis-candidacy edges, none of which cite this chapter/line.

edges.quote: “You know you cannot keep her,” Jon said gently, “no more than I could stay with Ygritte. You said the words, Sam, the same as I did. The same as all of us.”
edges.note: The video's preferred resolution: Jon's heroism should come from his own moral choices -- the mercy he learned from Ned and showed toward Ygritte -- rather than from blood or prophecy (substrate B82). Read as a thematic counterweight to the whole Jon-is-AA-chosen-one framing, parallel in function to how Z3/Z4 contradict the Stannis candidacy. The quote itself states Jon's mercy-driven choice, not an explicit AA repudiation; the contradiction is the theory's own interpretive argument.
